serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of Hainanese Chicken Rice weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of poached chicken, a mound of fragrant chicken-fat rice, sliced cucumber, and accompanying dipping sauces.

nutrient_source.title: Hainanese Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code EX000101
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Database entry for Hainanese Chicken Rice (Food Code EX000101). This is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white jasmine rice (cooked, boiled)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite Hainanese Chicken Rice dish. GI of 58 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white jasmine rice (cooked, boiled). The presence of chicken fat and protein in the dish moderates the overall glycaemic response relative to plain rice alone. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (58 × 100.8) / 100 ≈ 58.5, rounded to 58.8 as reported in source record.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based dipping sauces, seasoned chicken broth, and salted rice
hypertension.guidance: A standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 1566 mg sodium — about 68% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit — so individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce portions, choose low-sodium soy sauce variants, and limit serving frequency to avoid cumulative sodium overload.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycaemic index rice base with a high per-serving carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The jasmine rice base carries a GI of approximately 58 (medium), but a standard 450 g serving delivers roughly 101 g of carbohydrate, producing a substantial glycaemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce rice portion size, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from chicken skin and chicken fat used in rice preparation, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 100 g provides 1.7 g saturated fat and 348 mg sodium; over a full 450 g serving this equates to approximately 7.7 g saturated fat and 1566 mg sodium, which warrants portion control and removal of chicken skin for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean poached protein with anti-inflammatory ginger component
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Poached chicken is a lean protein source with low inflammatory potential, and ginger used in the rice preparation provides mild anti-inflammatory polyphenols, making this dish generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed without excess sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hainanese Chicken Rice is Singapore's most iconic hawker dish and is widely regarded as the national dish of Singapore. The dish consists of tender whole chicken that is poached at sub-boiling temperatures (approximately 80–90°C) to preserve maximum juiciness, then plunged into ice water to create a characteristic gelatinous layer between the skin and meat. The chicken is served sliced over fragrant rice that has been stir-fried in rendered chicken fat with ginger and garlic before being cooked by the absorption method in seasoned chicken broth with pandan leaves, producing a rich, aromatic grain. The dish is accompanied by a clear chicken soup, sliced cucumber, and three traditional dipping sauces: a fresh chilli-ginger paste, a grated ginger-in-chicken-fat condiment, and dark soy sauce. Per 100 g the dish provides 169 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and 22.4 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 348 mg. A standard hawker-centre plate weighs approximately 450 g and delivers around 760 kcal and 1566 mg sodium. The GI is approximately 58 (medium), moderated by the fat and protein content relative to plain rice. Clinically, the dish is a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its high per-serving carbohydrate load, and a high concern for hypertension due to sodium from sauces and broth. It is halal and dairy-free. The dish traces its origins to Hainanese immigrants in Singapore and is available at hawker centres, coffee shops, food courts, and dedicated chicken rice restaurants island-wide.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code EX000101), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for the closest analogous food (white jasmine rice) with documented estimation basis.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with the poaching preparation method and absence of hydrogenated fats. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1. Water content is not reported in the HPB source and is set to -1. The duplicate '1 serving (350g)' portion entry in the input has been collapsed to a single entry in the serving section; the default serving of 450 g (1 plate, HPB-verified) is used for GL calculation. Hindi local name 'हैनानी चिकन राइस' is a standard transliteration used in Indian-language food contexts; no authoritative Hindi culinary term exists for this dish. Vitamin K (0.8 mcg/100g), copper (0.06 mg/100g), and manganese (0.15 mg/100g) are present in the input with enriched_json provenance (confidence 0.9) and are retained as supporting micronutrient data but not mapped to RESULT template fields that lack corresponding keys; these values are noted here for completeness.
